民國105年_第1次_醫學(三)_1:
[Open Evidence]
PANCOAST TUMOR (Superior Sulcus Tumor)
Definition & Epidemiology
　•	Lung cancer arising at the apex of the lung that invades apical chest wall structures
　•	Accounts for 5% of all bronchogenic carcinomas
　•	Histology: Adenocarcinoma (2/3), squamous cell carcinoma, large cell carcinoma
TNM Staging
　•	T3: Invasion of T1/T2 nerve roots, stellate ganglion, or first rib
　•	T4: Involvement of C8 or higher nerve roots, brachial plexus (trunks/divisions/cords), subclavian vessels, vertebral bodies, lamina, or spinal canal
Clinical Presentation (Pancoast-Tobias Syndrome)
Four Classic Features
　1.	Shoulder and arm pain (most common)
　　o	Radiates along C8-T2 dermatomes
　　o	Ulnar forearm and hand distribution
　2.	Horner syndrome
　　o	Ipsilateral ptosis
　　o	Miosis
　　o	Facial anhidrosis
　　o	Mechanism: Stellate ganglion invasion
　3.	Hand muscle weakness and atrophy
　　o	Lower brachial plexus (C8-T1) involvement
　4.	Upper extremity edema
　　o	Subclavian or brachiocephalic vein compression
PANCOAST Mnemonic
　•	Pain (shoulder/arm) - most common symptom
　•	Apex of lung - location
　•	Nerve involvement (C8-T2) - dermatomal distribution
　•	C8-T1 weakness - hand muscle atrophy
　•	Ocular signs (Horner) - ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis
　•	Arm edema - vascular compression
　•	Stellate ganglion - Horner mechanism
　•	Trimodality therapy - standard treatment
Diagnostic Triad
　MRI (thoracic inlet) + CT (chest/abdomen) + PET/CT (staging)
Treatment Gold Standard
　Preoperative chemoradiation → Surgery → Adjuvant therapy
